text.title: c4-model Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for c4-model. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Always create a System Context diagram first. It is the mandatory starting point for any architecture discussion. Keep it to 5–10 elements: the system, 1–3 personas, and 2–5 external systems. Do not show technology choices at this level.
rule.rationale: Without a Context diagram, stakeholders argue about scope before discussing architecture. The Context diagram aligns everyone on what the system does, who uses it, and what external systems it integrates with — all without technology details that would confuse non-technical stakeholders.

rule.statement: Create a Container diagram to show the high-level technology decisions. Each container is a separately deployable or runnable unit. Show the primary technology choice and the communication protocol for each relationship.
rule.rationale: The Container diagram is the most useful diagram for software architects and developers. It shows what to deploy, what technology to use, and how the containers communicate — enough information to make infrastructure and service boundary decisions.

rule.statement: Create Component diagrams only for containers with complex internals that multiple teams work on. Show the major components inside one container and their responsibilities. Do not attempt to show all containers' internals on a single diagram.
rule.rationale: Component diagrams are scoped to one container. Creating a single diagram with components from multiple containers mixes abstraction levels and becomes unreadable. One Component diagram per container, created only when that container's internals need to be communicated.

rule.statement: Do not create Level 4 (Code) diagrams manually. Generate them from IDE tooling or class diagram generators. Manual code diagrams are out of date within days of creation.
rule.rationale: Code changes constantly. Any manually maintained class diagram becomes a liability — it shows the wrong structure and misleads readers. Auto-generation from the actual code is the only sustainable approach.

rule.statement: Create a System Landscape diagram when the organization owns multiple related systems that interact. Use it as an entry point before drilling into any individual System Context diagram.
rule.rationale: Large organizations operate dozens of systems that share users and data. The System Landscape provides the "Level 0" view — all systems and their broad relationships — so stakeholders can orient themselves before zooming into one system.

rule.statement: Create Dynamic diagrams to document the runtime interaction for one specific use case or user story. Use them to explain complex flows that are unclear from the static structure diagrams.
rule.rationale: Static diagrams (Context, Container, Component) show structure but not behavior. When a "place order" flow involves 7 services and a message broker, the static Container diagram cannot convey the sequence. Dynamic diagrams add time-ordered interaction without requiring full UML sequence diagram complexity.

rule.statement: Create Deployment diagrams to map containers to infrastructure. Show which containers run on which nodes (VMs, Kubernetes pods, serverless functions) and the deployment infrastructure topology. Create one diagram per environment (production, staging) when they differ.
rule.rationale: The Container diagram shows what to deploy; the Deployment diagram shows where it is deployed. Without a Deployment diagram, new team members cannot answer "what happens if this node fails?" or "which containers share a database server?".

rule.statement: Store diagrams as code in version control using Structurizr DSL, PlantUML C4, or Mermaid C4 — never as JPEG/PNG exports from draw.io. Diagram-as-code diffs in PR review; binary image exports do not.
rule.rationale: Architecture diagrams that are not in version control diverge from the actual architecture. Diagram-as-code enables PR review of architectural changes, generation in CI/CD, and searching by element name across diagrams.
